stages.title: The Problem: Open Lines
stages.description: During WWII, Allied leaders needed to coordinate across the Atlantic -- but long-distance phone calls traveled over radio links and cables that anyone could tap. Here's the original voice, and what it sounded like after traveling through a 1940s phone line: bandlimited to 300-3400 Hz, degraded by tube amplifier distortion, and buried under noise (SNR = 28 dB). Distorted, but perfectly intelligible. An eavesdropper on the line hears everything.

diagnostics.text:
Signal correlation (original vs telephone): -0.0981
SNR: 28 dB (noise power is 1/631th of signal)
Bandwidth: 300-3400 Hz (telephone standard)

stages.title: First Attempt: A-3 Scrambler
stages.description: The Allies' first solution was the A-3 scrambler -- flip the audio spectrum around a secret carrier frequency (2000 Hz) so low sounds become high and vice versa. Listen to the scrambled audio: it sounds like garbled alien noise. Surely no one could understand that? But the Germans could. By 1941, they were routinely cracking A-3 calls using spectral analysis. The "secret" was a single number (the carrier frequency), and speech has such distinctive spectral patterns that finding it was trivial. Listen to the cracked output -- the speech is recovered. The A-3 was security through obscurity, and it had failed. The Allies needed something fundamentally different.

diagnostics.text:
Carrier frequency used: 2000 Hz
Carrier found by cracker: 1541 Hz
Top candidates: 1541 Hz, 1745 Hz, 1663 Hz, 1622 Hz, 1500 Hz
Correlation (original vs scrambled): -0.0014
Correlation (original vs cracked): 0.0208

The cracker finds the carrier by trying different frequencies
and scoring how "speech-like" each result sounds (low spectral
centroid + high variance = speech). One parameter to guess.

stages.title: The Key Insight: Digitize First
stages.description: The A-3 failed because it scrambled the analog waveform -- the spectral shape of speech survived the transformation. SIGSALY's breakthrough was to first DIGITIZE the speech using a vocoder ("voice coder"). The vocoder splits audio into 10 frequency bands and measures the energy in each, 50 times per second. Each measurement is quantized to just 6 discrete levels (0-5). The result sounds robotic but is perfectly intelligible -- and crucially, it's now a stream of small integers that can be encrypted with mathematics.

diagnostics.text:
Frames: 285 at 50 fps (5.70s)
Voiced: 107/285 (38%), Unvoiced: 178/285 (62%)
Compression: 876:1 (44100 samples/sec -> 600 values/sec)
Quantization: 6 levels per band (10 bands)
Level distribution: L0=473, L1=542, L2=509, L3=500, L4=389, L5=437
Correlation (original vs vocoded): -0.0028

The robotic quality comes from coarse quantization (6 levels
vs ~65,000 in 16-bit audio) and simplified excitation (pulse train
instead of real glottal waveform).

stages.title: The Key: Vinyl Records of Random Noise
stages.description: To encrypt the vocoder's integer stream, SIGSALY needed a source of randomness that was truly unpredictable. Bell Labs generated random noise using mercury-vapor vacuum tubes -- the thermal chaos of hot ionized gas, sampled and quantized into values from 0 to 5. This noise was recorded onto vinyl phonograph records. Each record held 12 minutes of key material. Identical copies were pressed and shipped by armed military courier to each terminal -- one for Washington, one for London. Used records were destroyed immediately after the call. Listen to what a key record sounded like: pure noise. No pattern, no structure, no information -- just randomness. That's exactly what makes the encryption unbreakable.

diagnostics.text:
Key record: 536 frames at 50 fps (10.7s)
Total random values: 5,896 (536 frames x (10 bands + 1 pitch))
Each value: independently, uniformly random in [0, 5]

Three requirements for unbreakable encryption (all must hold):
  1. Key must be truly random (SIGSALY: vacuum tube thermal noise)
  2. Key must be at least as long as the message (12-min records)
  3. Key must never be reused (records destroyed after use)

stages.title: Encryption: Vocoder + Key = Noise
stages.description: Now SIGSALY combines the two pieces. For each vocoder parameter (a number from 0 to 5), it subtracts the corresponding key value using modular arithmetic: encrypted = (voice - key) mod 6. For example, if the voice level is 3 and the key value is 5: (3 - 5) mod 6 = -2 mod 6 = 4. The encrypted value (4) reveals nothing about the original (3) because the key (5) is random and unknown to the eavesdropper. Listen to the result -- the encrypted output is pure noise. Compare its spectrogram to the original: no speech structure remains. This is what travels over the phone line.

diagnostics.text:
Correlation (original vs encrypted audio): 0.0001 (should be ~0.0)
Correlation (original params vs encrypted params): 0.0066
Encrypted level distribution: L0=478, L1=486, L2=468, L3=450, L4=469, L5=499
  (Should be ~uniform: ~475 each)

The encrypted values are uniformly distributed -- every level (0-5)
appears with roughly equal frequency, regardless of what the original
speech contained. This is the hallmark of good encryption.

stages.title: Decryption: Recovering the Voice
stages.description: At the receiving terminal in London, the identical copy of the vinyl key record plays in perfect synchronization. Decryption is simply the inverse operation: add the key value back. decrypted = (encrypted + key) mod 6. Continuing the example: (4 + 5) mod 6 = 9 mod 6 = 3 -- the original value is recovered exactly. The decrypted parameters are fed into the vocoder's resynthesizer, which reconstructs audible speech. Listen: the voice is back, with the characteristic robotic quality of vocoded audio. This is what Churchill and Roosevelt actually heard during their secure wartime conversations.

diagnostics.text:
Roundtrip verification:
  Band levels:  PERFECT -- every value matches
  Pitch levels: PERFECT -- every value matches
  Total values checked: 2850 bands + 285 pitch = 3135

The one-time pad is perfectly reversible: subtract to encrypt,
add to decrypt. No information is lost -- every single parameter
is recovered exactly, bit for bit.

stages.title: Why It's Unbreakable
stages.description: What if the Germans applied their spectral analysis technique to SIGSALY? Listen: it fails completely. With A-3, the spectral shape of speech survived the transformation (just mirrored), so there was a pattern to find. With the one-time pad, each encrypted value is statistically independent of the original -- the key has 5,896 independently random values where A-3 had just one secret number. Claude Shannon proved in 1949 that a one-time pad achieves "perfect secrecy": knowing the ciphertext tells you literally nothing about the message, even with infinite computing power.

diagnostics.text:
Correlation (original vs crack attempt): 0.0036
Compare to successful A-3 crack: 0.0208

The spectral analysis technique that broke A-3 finds nothing here.
There is no single parameter to guess -- the key has 5,896
independent random values. Brute-forcing all possibilities would
require trying 6^5896 combinations -- a number
far larger than the atoms in the observable universe.

See: Shannon, C.E. (1949). "Communication Theory of Secrecy Systems."
https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~rist/642-spring-2014/shannon-secrecy.pdf

stages.title: The Engineering Challenge: Synchronization
stages.description: The one-time pad is mathematically perfect -- but it demands perfect logistics. Both terminals must play their vinyl key records at exactly the same speed, starting at exactly the same moment. The vocoder produces one frame every 20 milliseconds (50 frames per second). If the receiver's turntable drifts by even a single frame, it reads the wrong key value for that frame and every frame after it. Since adjacent key values are independently random, using the wrong one is no better than using a completely different key. Listen to what happens with increasing misalignment: 1 frame (20ms) sounds just as destroyed as 25 frames (500ms). This is why SIGSALY required precision time-of-day clocks at both terminals, and why the complete system weighed over 50 tons and filled 40 equipment racks. The key management problem -- manufacturing, duplicating, shipping by armed courier, synchronizing playback, and destroying used records -- was as critical to security as the mathematics.

diagnostics.text:
What is a "frame"?
  The vocoder samples speech 50 times per second.
  1 frame = 1/50th of a second = 20 milliseconds.
  That's roughly the duration of a single consonant sound.

Offset                Correct   Accuracy  Random Chance
-------------------- -------- ---------- --------------
0 (perfect sync)         2850     100.0%             --
1 frame(s) (20ms)         485      17.0%         ~16.7%
5 frame(s) (100ms)        478      16.8%         ~16.7%
25 frame(s) (500ms)       496      17.4%         ~16.7%

All offsets converge to ~16.7% accuracy = random chance (1/6).
Whether the turntable is off by 20ms or 2 full seconds,
the result is equally useless. The one-time pad is all-or-nothing.
